Scenario.title: Maroochy Water Breach: Multi-Stage Cyber Sabotage Against Wastewater Infrastructure
Scenario.overview: Water and Wastewater Systems Complex A sophisticated cyberattack targeting a water utility's SCADA systems, leveraging multi-stage exploitation to disrupt wastewater management operations and cause environmental damage.
Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Open Source Intelligence (OSINT), Internet-facing assets, Malicious Payload, Service, Network Traffic, OS Process, Active Directory, Boot Sector
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: DNS/Passive DNS, Vulnerabilities, General Access Method, Command and Scripting Interpreter, Service Creation (Mechanism), Network Traffic Flow (Mechanism), Scan Databases, Network Traffic Content (Mechanism), Reg.exe Utility, Data Manipulation, Custom Command and Control Protocol, Modify System Partition

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.description: The attacker performs passive DNS analysis to gather publicly available information about the water utility's infrastructure, including vendor details and network configurations, identifying potential vulnerabilities in legacy SCADA systems.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.description: The threat actor identifies weaknesses in outdated industrial control protocols and SCADA systems through open-source research, preparing custom exploits to compromise the target's network.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.description: The attacker exploits unpatched remote maintenance tools with known vulnerabilities in the utility's perimeter defenses to establish an initial foothold in the network.

stage.name: Execution
stage.description: Malicious scripts are executed to manipulate control system interfaces, allowing the attacker to override process parameters and initiate unauthorized operations.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.technique_name: T1543: Create or Modify System Process
stage.description: The attacker creates a persistent backdoor by modifying SCADA system service configurations with hardcoded credentials, ensuring continued access despite reboots.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.capec: CAPEC-555: Remote Services with Stolen Credentials
stage.description: Stolen administrative credentials are used to escalate privileges, granting access to high-value targets within the control network.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.description: The attacker encrypts command-and-control (C2) communications to avoid detection by network monitoring tools while mapping the network topology.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.capec: CAPEC-70: Try Common or Default Usernames and Passwords
stage.description: Keylogging malware is deployed to steal operator credentials from compromised workstations, enabling further lateral movement.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.description: Network sniffing tools like Reg.exe are used to identify critical control system components and redundant pathways, facilitating targeted attacks.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.description: The attacker moves laterally using RDP/SSH to access isolated control system segments, expanding their footprint across the network.

stage.name: Collection
stage.description: Operational data is extracted to determine optimal timing for sabotage, ensuring maximum disruption to wastewater treatment processes.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.technique_name: T1094: Custom Command and Control Protocol
stage.description: Stolen credentials and system state data are exfiltrated to external C2 servers using a custom protocol to avoid detection by traditional security measures.

stage.name: Impact
stage.description: The attacker modifies boot sector firmware to alter pump settings and valve controls, causing wastewater overflow and environmental contamination.

Scenario.summary: A coordinated cyberattack on a water utility's SCADA systems, combining reconnaissance, persistence, privilege escalation, and sabotage to disrupt wastewater management and cause environmental harm. The attack leverages both known vulnerabilities and custom exploits to maintain stealth and maximize impact.
